speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Hence! Home, you idle creatures, get you home!

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Is this a holiday? What, know you not,

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Being mechanical, you ought not walk

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Upon a laboring day without the sign

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Of your profession?—Speak, what trade art thou?

speaker: CARPENTER
text: Why, sir, a carpenter.

speaker: MARULLUS
text: Where is thy leather apron and thy rule?

speaker: MARULLUS
text: What dost thou with thy best apparel on?—

speaker: MARULLUS
text: You, sir, what trade are you?

speaker: COBBLER
text: Truly, sir, in respect of a fine workman, I am

speaker: COBBLER
text: but, as you would say, a cobbler.

speaker: MARULLUS
text: But what trade art thou? Answer me directly.

speaker: COBBLER
text: A trade, sir, that I hope I may use with a safe

speaker: COBBLER
text: conscience, which is indeed, sir, a mender of bad

speaker: COBBLER
text: soles.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: What trade, thou knave? Thou naughty knave, what trade?

speaker: COBBLER
text: Nay, I beseech you, sir, be not out with me.

speaker: COBBLER
text: Yet if you be out, sir, I can mend you.

speaker: MARULLUS
text: What mean’st thou by that? Mend me, thou saucy fellow?

speaker: COBBLER
text: Why, sir, cobble you.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Thou art a cobbler, art thou?

speaker: COBBLER
text: Truly, sir, all that I live by is with the

speaker: COBBLER
text: awl. I meddle with no tradesman’s matters nor

speaker: COBBLER
text: women’s matters, but withal I am indeed, sir, a

speaker: COBBLER
text: surgeon to old shoes: when they are in great danger,

speaker: COBBLER
text: I recover them. As proper men as ever trod upon

speaker: COBBLER
text: neat’s leather have gone upon my handiwork.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: But wherefore art not in thy shop today?

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Why dost thou lead these men about the streets?

speaker: COBBLER
text: Truly, sir, to wear out their shoes, to

speaker: COBBLER
text: get myself into more work. But indeed, sir, we

speaker: COBBLER
text: make holiday to see Caesar and to rejoice in his

speaker: COBBLER
text: triumph.

speaker: MARULLUS
text: Wherefore rejoice? What conquest brings he home?

speaker: MARULLUS
text: What tributaries follow him to Rome

speaker: MARULLUS
text: To grace in captive bonds his chariot wheels?

speaker: MARULLUS
text: You blocks, you stones, you worse than senseless things!

speaker: MARULLUS
text: O you hard hearts, you cruel men of Rome,

speaker: MARULLUS
text: Knew you not Pompey? Many a time and oft

speaker: MARULLUS
text: Have you climbed up to walls and battlements,

speaker: MARULLUS
text: To towers and windows, yea, to chimney tops,

speaker: MARULLUS
text: Your infants in your arms, and there have sat

speaker: MARULLUS
text: The livelong day, with patient expectation,

speaker: MARULLUS
text: To see great Pompey pass the streets of Rome.

speaker: MARULLUS
text: And when you saw his chariot but appear,

speaker: MARULLUS
text: Have you not made an universal shout,

speaker: MARULLUS
text: That Tiber trembled underneath her banks

speaker: MARULLUS
text: To hear the replication of your sounds

speaker: MARULLUS
text: Made in her concave shores?

speaker: MARULLUS
text: And do you now put on your best attire?

speaker: MARULLUS
text: And do you now cull out a holiday?

speaker: MARULLUS
text: And do you now strew flowers in his way

speaker: MARULLUS
text: That comes in triumph over Pompey’s blood?

speaker: MARULLUS
text: Be gone!

speaker: MARULLUS
text: Run to your houses, fall upon your knees,

speaker: MARULLUS
text: Pray to the gods to intermit the plague

speaker: MARULLUS
text: That needs must light on this ingratitude.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Go, go, good countrymen, and for this fault

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Assemble all the poor men of your sort,

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Draw them to Tiber banks, and weep your tears

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Into the channel, till the lowest stream

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Do kiss the most exalted shores of all.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: See whe’er their basest mettle be not moved.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: They vanish tongue-tied in their guiltiness.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Go you down that way towards the Capitol.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: This way will I. Disrobe the images

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: If you do find them decked with ceremonies.

speaker: MARULLUS
text: May we do so?

speaker: MARULLUS
text: You know it is the feast of Lupercal.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: It is no matter. Let no images

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Be hung with Caesar’s trophies. I’ll about

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: And drive away the vulgar from the streets;

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: So do you too, where you perceive them thick.

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: These growing feathers plucked from Caesar’s wing

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Will make him fly an ordinary pitch,

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: Who else would soar above the view of men

speaker: FLAVIUS
text: And keep us all in servile fearfulness.
